topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the room felt thick, suffocating. I slammed my textbook shut, the sound echoing sharply in the small space. My gaze snapped to Liam, who was lounging on his bed, a book open in his lap. It was my journal. The familiar, private script, the raw outpouring of my anxieties and triumphs, was visible, illuminated by the harsh overhead light. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outward into a prickly heat across my skin. I didn't move, just stared, my jaw clenched so tight my teeth ached. When he finally looked up, his expression was one of mild surprise, quickly morphing into a hesitant, almost apologetic shrug. "Oh, hey. Didn't realize you were reading that." His tone was too light, a careless dismissal that made my hands tremble slightly as I slowly, deliberately, reached for the book.

[story 2]

I found myself pacing the cramped confines of our shared dorm room, a restless energy vibrating beneath my skin. Every glance toward Liam felt like a physical assault. I watched him from across the room, noting the slight, almost unconscious way he turned a page, the way his eyes lingered just a fraction too long on the ink bleeding onto the page. My breath hitched in my throat; it felt like sandpaper scraping against my windpipe. When he finally lowered the book, his eyes met mine, and there was a flicker—a momentary confusion, perhaps, quickly masked by a defensive tightening around his mouth. "What's up?" he asked, his voice pitched a little too high, a clear attempt to deflect the uncomfortable silence that had settled between us. I just shook my head, refusing to meet his gaze, focusing instead on the scuff marks on the floor.

[story 3]

The silence between us was a weapon. It stretched taut, vibrating with unspoken accusations. I felt a distinct hardening in my chest, a refusal to soften or yield an inch. I watched Liam carefully as he turned a page—a slow, deliberate action that seemed entirely too casual given the context. My knuckles turned white where I gripped my water bottle. A low, sharp sound escaped my throat, more of a hiss than a word. When he finally closed the book, he looked genuinely startled, his casual demeanor dissolving into something guarded. "Whoa, what's wrong?" he asked, his voice laced with an uncertain concern. I didn't answer immediately. I just stood there, radiating a palpable tension, my posture rigid, daring him to address the undeniable fact of what he had just done.
